title: Granular Superconductivity in La$_{2}$PrNi$_{2}$O$_{7-δ}$ Thin Films
summary: Superconductivity realized in bilayer nickelate thin films enables direct spectroscopic and transport studies at ambient pressure. However, a persistent two-step resistive transition remains a major barrier to achieving optimal superconducting properties. Here, we show that the two-step transition in La$_2$PrNi$_2$O$_{7-δ}$ thin films originates from the granular nature of superconductivity, specifically, the coexistence of two distinct superconducting grain phases coupled by a Josephson junction network. A secondary, lower-temperature transition appears in the $R(T)$ curve, even when residual resistance becomes vanishingly small near 30 K. This two-step behavior significantly lowers the zero-resistance transition temperature, $T_{c, zero}$$\approx$ 10 K, and limits advanced spectroscopic studies. Our findings reveal the microscopic mechanism underlying the two-step transition in thin films and underscore the need for improved oxygen homogeneity to achieve bulk superconductivity in this system.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the origin of a two-step resistive transition in La₂PrNi₂O₇₋δ bilayer nickelate thin films, revealing it stems from granular superconductivity with two distinct superconducting grain phases coupled via Josephson junction networks, limiting Tc_zero to ~10K.
AI.motivation: The persistent two-step resistive transition in bilayer nickelate superconductors has been a major barrier to achieving optimal superconducting properties, limiting critical temperature and preventing advanced spectroscopic studies at ambient pressure.
AI.method: Transport and spectroscopic studies on La₂PrNi₂O₇₋δ thin films at ambient pressure, analyzing the temperature-dependent resistance R(T) curves and their correlation with granular phase behavior.
AI.result: The two-step transition originates from granular superconductivity - coexistence of two distinct superconducting grain phases coupled by Josephson junction networks. A secondary lower-temperature transition appears in R(T) even when residual resistance becomes vanishingly small near 30K, significantly reducing Tc_zero to approximately 10K.
AI.conclusion: The microscopic mechanism is the granular nature of superconductivity in these thin films. Achieving bulk superconductivity requires improved oxygen homogeneity to eliminate the Josephson junction coupling effects between superconducting grains.

title: Spectral solution of axisymmetric magnetization problems for thin superconducting shells
summary: Existing numerical methods for modeling magnetization in thin type-II superconducting films have mostly been developed for flat films. This work introduces an efficient spectral method for axisymmetric magnetization problems involving non-flat films. The method is based on the integral thin-shell current-density formulation of the problem, employs Chebyshev polynomial expansions for spatial discretization, and uses the method of lines for time integration. It applies to both open and closed axisymmetric shells and is so accurate that the solutions obtained can serve as benchmarks for numerical methods for general, not necessarily axisymmetric, thin-shell magnetization problems. As one of the examples, we consider magnetic shielding by a superconducting sphere.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an efficient spectral method for modeling magnetization in thin type-II superconducting films that are non-flat and axisymmetric. The method uses Chebyshev polynomial expansions for spatial discretization and the method of lines for time integration, based on an integral thin-shell current-density formulation.
AI.motivation: Existing numerical methods for magnetization modeling in thin type-II superconducting films have mostly been developed for flat films. There is a need for efficient methods to handle non-flat films, particularly for axisymmetric magnetization problems.
AI.method: The method is based on the integral thin-shell current-density formulation of the problem. It employs Chebyshev polynomial expansions for spatial discretization and uses the method of lines for time integration. The approach applies to both open and closed axisymmetric shells.
AI.result: The method is highly accurate, achieving solutions that can serve as benchmarks for numerical methods for general thin-shell magnetization problems. An example application demonstrates magnetic shielding by a superconducting sphere.
AI.conclusion: The proposed spectral method provides an efficient and accurate approach for axisymmetric magnetization problems involving non-flat superconducting films, offering benchmark-quality results for both open and closed axisymmetric shells.

title: Exploring the conventional and anomalous Josephson effects at arbitrary disorder strength in systems with spin-dependent fields
summary: We present a theory of the Josephson current in superconductor-normal metal-superconductor (SNS) junctions in the presence of generic spin-dependent fields, such as spin-orbit coupling (SOC), Zeeman fields, and altermagnetism. We consider systems with arbitrary disorder strength, going beyond the usual diffusive and ballistic approximations. Using the linearized quasiclassical Eilenberger equation, we derive a compact expression for the Josephson current, which is then applied to various situations of experimental interest. First, we investigate the evolution of the Josephson critical current in an applied magnetic field in the presence of Rashba and Dresselhaus SOC, and discuss how this dependence can be used to probe SOC in the junction. We then study the anomalous Josephson ($\varphi_0$) effect in systems with Rashba SOC and show that it remains robust over a wide range of disorder strength, and can even be enhanced by moderate disorder in sufficiently long junctions. Finally, we investigate the Josephson current in disordered junctions with altermagnets, and show how the $0$-$π$ transition in such systems is suppressed by disorder. Our results may be useful for describing experimental setups with high-mobility samples, which nevertheless always contain some amount of disorder, and where neither purely ballistic nor diffusive approximations are adequate.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a theory for Josephson current in SNS junctions with spin-dependent fields (SOC, Zeeman fields, altermagnetism) applicable to arbitrary disorder strength, beyond standard diffusive/ballistic approximations. It analyzes magnetic field dependence of critical current, anomalous Josephson φ₀ effect, and 0-π transitions in altermagnets.
AI.motivation: Real experimental setups with high-mobility samples always contain some disorder, yet existing theories are limited to either purely ballistic or purely diffusive limits. There was a need for a unified theory covering the intermediate disorder regime.
AI.method: Using the linearized quasiclassical Eilenberger equation, the authors derived a compact expression for the Josephson current applicable to arbitrary disorder strength, then applied it to three specific scenarios of experimental interest.
AI.result: Three key findings: (1) Magnetic field dependence of critical current with Rashba/Dresselhaus SOC can serve as a probe for SOC; (2) Anomalous Josephson φ₀ effect remains robust over wide disorder range and can be enhanced by moderate disorder in long junctions; (3) 0-π transition in altermagnet-based junctions is suppressed by disorder.
AI.conclusion: This unified theory fills the gap between ballistic and diffusive limits, providing a practical framework for analyzing experiments on high-mobility samples with residual disorder, relevant for probing SOC and designing Josephson junction-based devices.

title: Topological multicomponent-pairing superconductivity in twisted bilayer cuprates
summary: We investigate the emergence of a multicomponent superconducting state in twisted bilayer cuprates, characterized by the order parameter $s+d_1 e^{iφ_1}+d_2 e^{iφ_2}$, where $s=s_1+s_2$ denotes the symmetric combination of the layer-resolved $s$-wave components, and $s_i$ and $d_i$ ($i=1,2$) represent the $s$-wave and $d$-wave pairings associated with the individual layers. In particular, when $φ_1-φ_2\neq 0,π$, this three-component pairing state is topologically nontrivial. By combining Ginzburg--Landau analysis with self-consistent mean-field calculations based on a microscopic model, we show that such a topological three-component pairing state can be stabilized over a substantial parameter regime. Our results indicate that twisted bilayer cuprates can remain chiral and topological even in the presence of a sizable $s$-wave pairing component.

AI.tldr: This paper studies a multicomponent superconducting state in twisted bilayer cuprates with order parameter s + d₁e^(iφ₁) + d₂e^(iφ₂). When φ₁-φ₂ ≠ 0,π, this three-component pairing state becomes topologically nontrivial. Using Ginzburg-Landau theory and mean-field calculations, the authors show this topological state can be stabilized in a substantial parameter regime, and twisted bilayer cuprates can remain chiral and topological even with sizable s-wave pairing.
AI.motivation: Investigate the emergence of a multicomponent superconducting state in twisted bilayer cuprates, particularly when the phase difference φ₁-φ₂ ≠ 0,π, which gives rise to topological nontrivial properties.
AI.method: Combined Ginzburg-Landau analysis with self-consistent mean-field calculations based on a microscopic model.
AI.result: The topological three-component pairing state (s + d₁e^(iφ₁) + d₂e^(iφ₂)) can be stabilized over a substantial parameter regime. The state remains chiral and topological even with sizable s-wave pairing.
AI.conclusion: Twisted bilayer cuprates can remain chiral and topological even in the presence of a sizable s-wave pairing component. This three-component pairing state is stabilized over a substantial parameter regime.

title: K$_2$Co$_2$(TeO$_{3}$)$_{3}$ $\cdot$ 2.5 H$_2$O : A mineral-inspired pseudo-honeycomb cobalt dimer antiferromagnet
comment: Main text (21 pages, 5 figures, 1 table); Supplementary information (27 pages, 8 figures, 18 tables)
summary: In recent years, magnetically-frustrated triangular and honeycomb lattice cobaltates have seen extensive study in the pursuit of a quantum spin liquid (QSL) state in a real material. In this work, we describe the hydroflux synthesis of K$_2$Co$_2$(TeO$_{3}$)$_{3}$ $\cdot$ 2.5 H$_2$O (KCoTOH), a novel zemannite-type antiferromagnet (AFM) possessing structural elements of both triangular dimer and honeycomb structural motifs. Bulk magnetometry and specific heat data support the onset of long-range AFM order below $T_\text{N}$ = 7.6(1) K, with neutron diffraction and muon spin relaxation ($μ$SR) measurements placing the majority of the ordered moment within the pseudo-honeycomb plane. We resolve three unique oscillation frequencies from the zero-field $μ$SR spectra, additionally suggesting a remarkably low level of structural disorder in as-grown KCoTOH crystals. Whereas interactions between dimerized chains of Co$^{2+}$ cations are typically observed to be negligible or ferromagnetic in nature, the largely planar ordering motif observed in KCoTOH is instead stabilized by net antiferromagnetic interactions through bridging tellurite groups. This work highlights the potential of hydroflux synthesis methods in the stabilization of magnetic materials possessing novel and potentially more frustrated lattice geometries.

AI.tldr: Synthesis and characterization of K₂Co₂(TeO₃)₃ · 2.5 H₂O (KCoTOH), a novel zemannite-type antiferromagnet with triangular dimer and honeycomb structural motifs, exhibiting long-range AFM order below T_N = 7.6 K.
AI.motivation: The pursuit of quantum spin liquid (QSL) states in real materials has driven extensive study of magnetically-frustrated triangular and honeycomb lattice cobaltates. This work aims to discover new materials with potentially more frustrated lattice geometries through hydroflux synthesis methods.
AI.method: Hydroflux synthesis was used to grow KCoTOH crystals. Characterization included bulk magnetometry, specific heat measurements, neutron diffraction, and muon spin relaxation (μSR) spectroscopy.
AI.result: Long-range AFM order was confirmed below T_N = 7.6(1) K. Neutron diffraction and μSR measurements placed the majority of ordered moment within the pseudo-honeycomb plane. Three unique oscillation frequencies were resolved from zero-field μSR spectra, indicating remarkably low structural disorder. Unlike typical dimerized chain cobaltates with negligible or ferromagnetic inter-chain interactions, KCoTOH features antiferromagnetic interactions through bridging tellurite groups stabilizing the planar ordering motif.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates the effectiveness of hydroflux synthesis in stabilizing magnetic materials with novel frustrated lattice geometries. The discovery of KCoTOH provides a new platform for studying geometric frustration and quantum magnetism in materials combining triangular dimer and honeycomb structural elements.

title: Stability of Supported Pd-based Ethanol Oxidation Reaction Electrocatalysts in Alkaline Media
summary: This study evaluates the dissolution of the supported electrocatalysts Pd/C, PdSn/C, PdNb/C, and PdFe3O4/C during ethanol oxidation reaction for ADLFC applications. A scanning flow cell (SFC) coupled to an inductively coupled mass spectrometry (online ICP-MS) is used to assess the dissolution stability in a broad potential window. Accelerated stress tests with and without ethanol are developed using a rotating disk electrode (RDE) with dissolution products analysis by ex-situ ICP-MS. Potential profiles simulating those experienced by the catalyst during regular fuel cell operation were used. Sn and Fe catalysts demonstrate improved activity and stability compared with the material with Pd alone. For these reasons, PdSn/C and PdFe3O4/C are suitable for ADLFC applications. Severe Nb dissolution destabilizes Pd, increasing its leaching. This work demonstrates that while additional metals and oxides can improve the alcohol oxidation kinetics of Pd, these additives' dissolution stability must already be considered at the catalyst design stage.

AI.tldr: This study evaluates dissolution stability of Pd-based electrocatalysts (Pd/C, PdSn/C, PdNb/C, PdFe3O4/C) for ethanol oxidation in ADLFC applications using SFC-ICP-MS and RDE techniques. PdSn/C and PdFe3O4/C show improved activity and stability, while Nb causes severe Pd leaching. Dissolution stability of additives should be considered at catalyst design stage.
AI.motivation: To evaluate the dissolution behavior of supported electrocatalysts (Pd/C, PdSn/C, PdNb/C, and PdFe3O4/C) during ethanol oxidation reaction for anion exchange membrane fuel cell (ADLFC) applications.
AI.method: Scanning flow cell (SFC) coupled to inductively coupled mass spectrometry (online ICP-MS) was used to assess dissolution stability in a broad potential window. Accelerated stress tests with and without ethanol were developed using a rotating disk electrode (RDE) with dissolution products analysis by ex-situ ICP-MS. Potential profiles simulating fuel cell operation were used.
AI.result: Sn and Fe catalysts demonstrated improved activity and stability compared to Pd alone. PdSn/C and PdFe3O4/C are suitable for ADLFC applications. Severe Nb dissolution destabilizes Pd, increasing its leaching.
AI.conclusion: Additional metals and oxides (Sn, Fe) can improve alcohol oxidation kinetics of Pd, but their dissolution stability must be considered at the catalyst design stage. Severe Nb dissolution destabilizes Pd, increasing its leaching.

title: d-Wave pair density wave superconductivity in a two-orbital model
summary: Motivated by exploring superconductivity in multi-orbital systems, we study two orbital models of spinful fermions representing ($p_x,p_y$) or ($d_{xz}, d_{yz})$ orbitals on the square lattice. For minimal interorbital $t$-$J$ or $t$-$V$ on-site interactions, a random phase approximation uncovers regimes of instability towards incommensurate $d_{xy}$ pair density wave ($d$-PDW) superconductivity with driven by interband pairing. We study the competition of PDW order with uniform nodal $d_{xy}$ pairing states and magnetic and charge density wave (CDW) instabilities. At strong coupling, we derive an effective hard-core Cooper pair Hamiltonian which we study using a bosonic Gutzwiller ansatz to reveal a period-$2$ PDW over a wide range of fillings as well as a checkerboard CDW at quarter-filling. Our results apply to correlated multi-orbital materials with quasi-1D bands, Hubbard models on the square-octagon lattice, and atomic fermions in $p$-orbitals. Our work highlights the role of the orbital content and multiband Fermi surfaces in stabilizing interband PDW states.

AI.tldr: This paper studies superconductivity in multi-orbital systems (p_x,p_y or d_xz,d_yz orbitals) on square lattice using RPA and bosonic Gutzwiller ansatz. It discovers interband-driven incommensurate d_xy pair density wave (d-PDW) states that compete with uniform pairing, magnetic, and CDW instabilities, showing that orbital content and multiband Fermi surfaces stabilize these novel PDW states.
AI.motivation: Exploring superconductivity in multi-orbital systems, specifically studying two orbital models of spinful fermions representing (p_x, p_y) or (d_xz, d_yz) orbitals on the square lattice with minimal interorbital t-J or t-V on-site interactions.
AI.method: Random Phase Approximation (RPA) for uncovering instability regimes; derivation of effective hard-core Cooper pair Hamiltonian at strong coupling; bosonic Gutzwiller ansatz for studying the model.
AI.result: Discovered regimes of instability towards incommensurate d_xy pair density wave (d-PDW) superconductivity driven by interband pairing. Found competition between PDW order and uniform nodal d_xy pairing states, magnetic and charge density wave (CDW) instabilities. Revealed period-2 PDW over wide range of fillings and checkerboard CDW at quarter-filling.
AI.conclusion: The orbital content and multiband Fermi surfaces play a key role in stabilizing interband PDW states. Results apply to correlated multi-orbital materials with quasi-1D bands, Hubbard models on square-octagon lattice, and atomic fermions in p-orbitals.

title: Laterally Differentiated Polymorphs: a route to multifunctional nanostructures
summary: Multifunctional materials can exhibit emergent behavior from the coupling of two or more different properties. For example, coupling between magnetic and ferroelectric order enables electrical control of the magnetic state, enabling for example magnetoelectric memory or logic devices that combine the nonvolatility of magnetic order with the energy efficiency of voltage control. Magnetic iron garnets have outstanding magnonic and magnetooptical properties making them valuable in a range of technologies, but they have not been successfully incorporated into thin film two-phase magnetoelectric nanocomposites. Taking advantage of heterogeneously patterned substrates, this work demonstrates the engineering of garnet-perovskite composites in which both phases are polymorphs with the same composition but dramatically different structures and properties. Applying an electric field to the perovskite phase modulates the magnon dispersion and magnetooptical response of the garnet, opening a path to voltage-controlled garnet devices.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates engineering of garnet-perovskite nanocomposites enabling electric field control of magnonic and magnetooptical properties in magnetic iron garnets.
AI.motivation: The research aims to incorporate magnetic iron garnets (which have excellent magnonic and magnetooptical properties) into thin film two-phase magnetoelectric nanocomposites. Previous attempts have failed, and the goal is to enable electrical control of magnetic states for voltage-controlled devices.
AI.method: The work uses heterogeneously patterned substrates to engineer garnet-perovskite composites where both phases are polymorphs with the same composition but dramatically different crystal structures. An electric field is applied to the perovskite phase to modulate the garnet properties.
AI.result: Applying an electric field to the perovskite phase successfully modulates both the magnon dispersion and magnetooptical response of the garnet phase, demonstrating voltage control of magnetic properties.
AI.conclusion: This approach opens a pathway to voltage-controlled garnet devices for magnetoelectric memory, logic devices, and other applications combining nonvolatility with energy efficiency.

title: Fortuitous Universality of Bose-Kondo Impurities
summary: We use the fuzzy-sphere approach to study the Bose-Kondo impurity problem, namely a spin-$S$ impurity coupled to the $(2+1)$-dimensional $O(3)$ Wilson-Fisher CFT (Heisenberg universality class). We demonstrate that for $S=1/2,1,3/2$ the impurity flows to a distinct stable interacting conformal defect for each $S$. Using large-scale exact diagonalization and density-matrix renormalization group methods, we observe integer-spaced defect spectrum consistent with defect conformal symmetry and compute several low-lying defect primary operators as well as the RG monotonic $g$-function. Our findings show that despite sharing the same symmetry and anomaly, Bose-Kondo impurities flow to distinct stable infrared conformal fixed points, which we refer to as \emph{fortuitous universality}. We expect this fortuitous universality to persist for all $S$, extending to $S\rightarrow\infty$, with each spin-$S$ impurity flowing to its own stable infrared conformal fixed point.

AI.tldr: Study of Bose-Kondo impurity problem using fuzzy-sphere approach, where a spin-S impurity couples to 2+1-dimensional O(3) Wilson-Fisher CFT. For S=1/2, 1, 3/2, distinct stable interacting conformal defects emerge, demonstrating "fortuitous universality" where different spins flow to different IR fixed points despite sharing symmetry and anomaly.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand how impurity spins in the Bose-Kondo problem behave when coupled to a conformal field theory in 2+1 dimensions. The key question is whether different spin-S impurities flow to the same or different infrared fixed points, and what determines their behavior.
AI.method: The authors employ three main methods: (1) Fuzzy-sphere approach as the theoretical framework, (2) Large-scale exact diagonalization for numerical calculations, (3) Density-matrix renormalization group (DMRG) methods for the analysis. They observe integer-spaced defect spectrum consistent with defect conformal symmetry and compute low-lying defect primary operators and the RG monotonic g-function.
AI.result: For S=1/2, 1, and 3/2, each spin flows to a distinct stable interacting conformal defect. The spectrum shows integer spacing consistent with defect conformal symmetry. The g-function was computed as an RG monotonic quantity. Despite sharing the same symmetry and anomaly, the impurities flow to different IR conformal fixed points.
AI.conclusion: The discovery of "fortuitous universality" - that each spin-S impurity flows to its own stable infrared conformal fixed point. This is expected to persist for all S, extending to S→∞. The result challenges the conventional understanding that impurities with same symmetry should flow to same fixed point, showing that additional structure determines distinct IR behavior.

title: Impact of charge transition levels on grain boundary properties in acceptor doped oxide ceramics: A phase-field study
summary: Advanced doping strategies enable oxide ceramic functionalities by tailoring bulk defect chemistry and space-charge-layer (SCL) behavior at interfaces. Charge transition levels (CTLs), defined as the Fermi level at which a defect changes its stable charge state, play a central role. Their alignment governs bulk defect chemistry, while their bending within SCLs induces additional charge-state transitions. Incorporating CTLs is therefore essential for a consistent description of defect equilibria and SCL formation. In this work, we propose a defect-chemistry-consistent phase-field model explicitly coupled with CTLs to investigate their role in SCL evolution. The model includes multivalent oxygen vacancies, multivalent acceptor dopants, electrons, and holes. It is applied to Fe-doped SrTiO3 over wide ranges of oxygen partial pressure and temperature, capturing both symmetric SCLs at stationary grain boundaries and asymmetric SCLs during migration. Two distinct grain boundary types, slow and fast boundaries, emerge during migration, consistent with experimental observations. Simulations reveal that CTL-governed bulk defect chemistry, together with CTL-induced charge-state transitions within SCLs, critically determine SCL characteristics. Moreover, CTL-mediated hole transport is significantly faster than acceptor dopant diffusion, modulating solute drag and grain boundary kinetics. Finally, the model predicts grain boundary properties dependent on both thermal history and boundary type, with slow and fast boundaries exhibiting distinct behaviors. This framework links defect chemistry, Fermi level, CTLs, and grain boundary kinetics, providing new insights for designing oxide ceramics with tailored properties.

title: Orbital-Selective $d$-wave Superconductivity in the Two-Band $t$-$J$ Model: Possible Applications to La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$
summary: We investigate superconductivity in a two-band $t$-$J$ model consisting of an itinerant orbital (orbital-0) and a quasi-localized orbital (orbital-1) using variational Monte Carlo. A robust orbital-selective $d$-wave superconducting state is found to emerge exclusively from the itinerant orbital. An analysis of the superexchange energy hierarchy shows that the quasi-localized orbital-1 competes with superconductivity by favoring local inter-orbital bound states, which act as energy defects and disrupt phase coherence. Consistently, the superconducting order parameter is monotonically suppressed as the occupancy of orbital-1 increases. Motivated by superconductivity in nickelate La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$, these results highlight the essential role of multi-orbital physics beyond the single-band $t$-$J$ framework and point to a concrete route to enhance $T_c$: suppressing the involvement of localized $d_{z^2}$-derived orbitals.

AI.tldr: This study investigates superconductivity in a two-band t-J model with an itinerant orbital and a quasi-localized orbital using variational Monte Carlo. The research finds a robust orbital-selective d-wave superconducting state emerging exclusively from the itinerant orbital, while the quasi-localized orbital competes with superconductivity by forming local inter-orbital bound states that disrupt phase coherence.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand multi-orbital physics in correlated electron systems, particularly in nickelates like La3Ni2O7, beyond the single-band t-J framework. The study aims to clarify why certain orbital configurations favor or suppress superconductivity.
AI.method: Variational Monte Carlo method was employed to study a two-band t-J model consisting of an itinerant orbital (orbital-0) and a quasi-localized orbital (orbital-1).
AI.result: A robust orbital-selective d-wave superconducting state emerges exclusively from the itinerant orbital. The quasi-localized orbital-1 competes with superconductivity by favoring local inter-orbital bound states, which act as energy defects and disrupt phase coherence. The superconducting order parameter is monotonically suppressed as the occupancy of orbital-1 increases.
AI.conclusion: The results highlight the essential role of multi-orbital physics beyond the single-band t-J framework. The study points to a concrete route to enhance Tc: suppressing the involvement of localized d_z2-derived orbitals.

title: Directional Criticality and Higher-Order Flatness: Designing Van Hove Singularities in Three Dimensions
summary: Van Hove singularities (VHSs) play a pivotal role in driving correlated electronic phenomena. Traditional classifications focus only on critical points where the band gradient vanishes in all directions. Here we establish a unified classification of VHSs in three-dimensional systems, characterized by the number of vanishing gradient components and Hessian eigenvalues: ordinary ($M$-type), higher-order ($T_1$, $T_2$, $T_3$), noncritical ordinary ($N_0$, $N_1$, $N_2$), and noncritical higher-order ($S_1$, $S_2$) types. Noncritical VHSs exhibit directional quenching: the gradient vanishes in a two-dimensional subspace while remaining finite along the orthogonal direction, yielding finite density-of-states enhancements with distinct energy dependencies. Using an $s$-orbital tight-binding model on the pyrochlore lattice with spin-orbit coupling, we demonstrate that all singularity classes emerge at distinct high-symmetry points through controlled tuning of the hopping ratio. This work establishes directional criticality and higher-order flatness as design principles for tailoring density-of-states enhancements in three-dimensional quantum materials.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes a unified classification of Van Hove singularities (VHSs) in 3D systems, introducing new categories beyond traditional critical points. The classification includes ordinary (M-type), higher-order (T1, T2, T3), noncritical ordinary (N0, N1, N2), and noncritical higher-order (S1, S2) types, based on the number of vanishing gradient components and Hessian eigenvalues. Noncritical VHSs exhibit directional quenching where the gradient vanishes in a 2D subspace while remaining finite along the orthogonal direction, yielding finite DOS enhancements with distinct energy dependencies. Using an s-orbital tight-binding model on the pyrochlore lattice with spin-orbit coupling, all singularity classes are demonstrated to emerge at distinct high-symmetry points through controlled tuning of the hopping ratio.
AI.motivation: Traditional VHS classifications only focus on critical points where the band gradient vanishes in all directions. However, this limited classification misses important phenomena in 3D systems where directional criticality can produce distinct density-of-states enhancements. The authors aim to establish a more comprehensive classification framework that can serve as design principles for tailoring electronic properties in quantum materials.
AI.method: The authors employ an s-orbital tight-binding model on the pyrochlore lattice with spin-orbit coupling. They systematically explore different high-symmetry points in the Brillouin zone and control the hopping ratio to generate all classes of VHSs. The classification is based on analyzing the number of vanishing gradient components (0, 1, 2, or 3) and the eigenvalues of the Hessian matrix.
AI.result: A complete unified classification of VHSs in 3D systems was established, including eight distinct types: M-type (ordinary), T1/T2/T3 (higher-order), N0/N1/N2 (noncritical ordinary), and S1/S2 (noncritical higher-order). All singularity classes were demonstrated to emerge at distinct high-symmetry points in the pyrochlore lattice model through controlled tuning of the hopping ratio. Noncritical VHSs show directional quenching with finite DOS enhancements having distinct energy dependencies.
AI.conclusion: Directional criticality and higher-order flatness serve as new design principles for tailoring density-of-states enhancements in three-dimensional quantum materials. This unified classification framework provides a systematic approach for understanding and engineering electronic correlations in complex 3D materials by leveraging different types of Van Hove singularities.

title: Optomagnetic non-thermal modification of the ferromagnetic resonance
summary: We investigate the photoinduced shift of the ferromagnetic resonance (FMR) frequency in magnets caused by the inverse Cotton-Mouton effect (ICME) under linearly polarized light. Using a Lagrangian description of magnetization dynamics, we derive the equations of motion, and obtain analytical expressions for the resonance frequency in both in-plane and out-of-plane equilibrium configurations. The theory shows that the FMR frequency depends on the polarization angle and propagation direction of light, with ICME producing a frequency shift that can dominate over thermal effects. The analytical results agree well with numerical simulations and with available experimental data for bismuth-substituted yttrium iron garnet, enabling estimation of the ICME contribution. These findings demonstrate that linearly polarized light can be used to control ferromagnetic resonance through magneto-optical effects.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how linearly polarized light can shift the ferromagnetic resonance (FMR) frequency in magnetic materials through the inverse Cotton-Mouton effect (ICME).
AI.motivation: To understand and demonstrate that light can be used to control magnetic resonance properties via magneto-optical effects, potentially enabling novel light-based magnetic switching or tuning mechanisms.
AI.method: The authors use a Lagrangian description of magnetization dynamics to derive equations of motion and obtain analytical expressions for resonance frequency in both in-plane and out-of-plane equilibrium configurations. They validate results against numerical simulations and experimental data on bismuth-substituted yttrium iron garnet.
AI.result: The FMR frequency depends on both the polarization angle and propagation direction of light. The ICME produces a frequency shift that can dominate over thermal effects. The analytical results agree well with numerical simulations and available experimental data.
AI.conclusion: Linearly polarized light can be used to control ferromagnetic resonance through magneto-optical effects (ICME), offering a potential method for optical manipulation of magnetic properties.

title: Fluctuation Mechanism of Single-Ion Anisotropy of Topological Insulator MnBi$_2$Te$_4$
summary: We demonstrate that charge fluctuations induced by electron hopping, combined with spin-orbit coupling, lift the sixfold degeneracy of the orbital singlet $^{6}S$ of Mn ions in the topological insulator MnBi$_2$Te$_4$, resulting in single-ion anisotropy. To solve the problem, a multiplet representation is introduced for the creation operators of atomic-state fermions in terms of the operators describing transitions between many-body wavefunctions. Using the operator form of perturbation theory up to the second order, we derive expressions for the populations $n_M$ of Mn ion states with spin projections $M$ of the $^{6}S$ term and determine the single ion anisotropy constants. The calculations reveal that the fluctuation mechanism ensures the possibility of implementing the easy-axis anisotropy observed in MnBi$_2$Te$_4$. Notably, the range of anisotropy constants $D_2$ obtained by varying the model parameters includes the value $D_2 = -0.0095$ meV, required to reproduce the critical field of the spin-flop transition $H_{\text{sf}}$, known from the experiment. The proposed mechanism has a wide range of applicability for describing the anisotropy in compounds where the ground state of a magnetic ion in a weak crystal field is described by an orbital singlet.

AI.tldr: Charge fluctuations from electron hopping combined with spin-orbit coupling lift the sixfold degeneracy of the orbital singlet ⁶S of Mn ions in the topological insulator MnBi₂Te₄, creating single-ion anisotropy. Using multiplet representation and second-order perturbation theory, the authors derive population expressions for Mn ion states and determine anisotropy constants, finding D₂ = -0.0095 meV that matches experimental spin-flop transition field.
AI.motivation: The MnBi₂Te₄ topological insulator exhibits an easy-axis anisotropy critical for its magnetic properties, but the origin of this single-ion anisotropy was unclear. The sixfold degeneracy of the ⁶S orbital singlet of Mn ions needs to be explained.
AI.method: 1) Introduce multiplet representation for atomic-state fermion creation operators describing transitions between many-body wavefunctions. 2) Apply second-order perturbation theory to derive expressions for populations n_M of Mn ion states with spin projections M. 3) Calculate single ion anisotropy constants by varying model parameters.
AI.result: The fluctuation mechanism successfully explains the easy-axis anisotropy in MnBi₂Te₄. The derived anisotropy constant D₂ = -0.0095 meV reproduces the experimental critical field of the spin-flop transition H_sf.
AI.conclusion: The charge fluctuation mechanism combined with spin-orbit coupling provides a general explanation for anisotropy in magnetic compounds with orbital singlet ground states in weak crystal fields.

title: Strain continuously rotates the Néel vector in altermagnetic MnTe
summary: Altermagnetism has recently emerged as a distinct class of collinear antiferromagnets that break time-reversal symmetry, exhibiting a host of novel properties. Applied strain has attracted particular attention as a key tuning parameter for altermagnets. Although several experimental studies have demonstrated the preparation of single-domain states through a combination of applied strain and magnetic field, the route to such states remains unclear. Here, we use magneto-optical measurements on single crystals of MnTe under applied strain to show that, in contrast to previous reports, strain acts primarily to rotate the Néel vector L continuously. Since the orientation of L determines the magnetic point group symmetry, this continuous rotation effectively tunes the symmetry and its associated physical properties. Furthermore, we demonstrate that built-in strain in free-standing crystals is sufficient to pin L into continuous textures over millimeter length scales. Together, these results provide guidance for future device design and open the door to leveraging the Néel vector orientation as a tunable degree of freedom in spintronic applications.

AI.tldr: Study reveals that strain continuously rotates the Néel vector in MnTe altermagnet, enabling tunable symmetry and millimeter-scale magnetic textures for spintronics applications.
AI.motivation: Previous experimental studies demonstrated that strain combined with magnetic field can prepare single-domain states in altermagnets, but the underlying mechanism was unclear. This work aims to understand how strain actually affects the magnetic order.
AI.method: Magneto-optical measurements on single crystals of MnTe under applied strain
AI.result: Strain acts primarily to rotate the Néel vector L continuously rather than just preparing single-domain states. Built-in strain in free-standing crystals can pin L into continuous textures over millimeter length scales.
AI.conclusion: The Néel vector orientation effectively tunes the magnetic point group symmetry and associated physical properties. This provides guidance for device design and opens possibilities for leveraging the Néel vector orientation as a tunable degree of freedom in spintronic applications.

title: Hubbard vs. Emery model: spectra, transport and relevance for cuprates
summary: Understanding the transport properties of cuprate superconductors is one of the central challenges in the physics of strongly correlated electrons. The most common approach is to define and solve a low-energy lattice model, but it is still unclear what the minimal model is to capture all relevant mechanisms and provide quantitative predictions. The main uncertainty concerns the choice of the orbital degrees of freedom to be included in the model, as well as the definition of the effective coupling. In this paper, we study the two most commonly considered models, namely the single-orbital Hubbard model and the three-orbital Emery model. We investigate and compare their spectral and transport properties, and find that the two models present a similar, but not the same, physical picture. We identify several strong quantitative differences which might allow one to discriminate between the two models by comparing theory with experiments. We compare our results for several physical quantities with 7 different experiments on 3 different La$_2$CuO$_4$-based cuprates, and in general find excellent agreement. The dc resistivity and the effective mass results suggest that the coupling constant in the effective Hubbard model is larger than expected. We find several more properties that are sensitive to the precise value of the coupling constant, including the critical doping for the Lifshitz transition, and the local spectral weight in the vicinity of the Fermi level; the latter provides a promising way to estimate the effective coupling constant in future photoemission experiments.

AI.tldr: This paper compares the single-orbital Hubbard model and three-orbital Emery model for cuprate superconductors, finding similar but quantitatively different physical pictures. Experiments suggest the effective coupling constant is larger than expected. Several measurable quantities can distinguish between the models, with local spectral weight near the Fermi level being a promising experimental probe.
AI.motivation: The central challenge is to determine the minimal model to capture all relevant mechanisms for cuprate superconductors and provide quantitative predictions. Key uncertainties concern the choice of orbital degrees of freedom and definition of effective coupling.
AI.method: The authors investigated and compared spectral and transport properties of both the single-orbital Hubbard model and the three-orbital Emery model, and compared results with 7 different experiments on 3 different La2CuO4-based cuprates.
AI.result: The two models present a similar but not identical physical picture. Strong quantitative differences were identified that could discriminate between the models via experiments. Excellent agreement was found with experiments. The dc resistivity and effective mass suggest the coupling constant in the effective Hubbard model is larger than expected. Properties sensitive to coupling constant include critical doping for Lifshitz transition and local spectral weight near Fermi level.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that while the single-orbital Hubbard model and three-orbital Emery model provide similar physical pictures for cuprate superconductors, they exhibit strong quantitative differences. The effective coupling constant in the effective Hubbard model is found to be larger than expected. Several properties are sensitive to the coupling value, including the critical doping for Lifshitz transition and local spectral weight near Fermi level. Excellent agreement was found with 7 experiments on 3 different La2CuO4-based cuprates. The local spectral weight near Fermi level offers a promising method to estimate effective coupling constants in future photoemission experiments.

title: Symmetry-guided and AI-accelerated design of intercalated transition metal dichalcogenides for antiferromagnetic spintronics
summary: The advancement of antiferromagnetic spintronics depends on quantum materials with target symmetry-dictated functionalities, however, their systematic discovery is hindered by the immense configurational complexity of the available material space. Here, we introduce a symmetry-guided, AI-accelerated framework incorporating graph neural networks with high generalization ability to overcome this bottleneck. Based on fully intercalated transition metal dichalcogenides (iTMDs) and using only 200 relaxed partially intercalated structures for transfer learning, our model effectively explores more than 100,000 partially intercalated configurations and identifies 35 altermagnetic and 20 $Tτ$-antiferromagnetic ground-state candidates. Interestingly, we show that tuning spin-group symmetry through intercalant arrangement or magnetic ordering realizes a series of d-wave altermagnets in these hexagonal systems with high spin-charge conversion efficiency. Furthermore, we reveal plentiful $Tτ$-antiferromagnets enabling efficient Néel spin-orbit torque switching, driven by giant $T$-odd spin Edelstein susceptibilities. These results establish iTMDs as a versatile platform for spintronics and provide a general strategy for the accelerated design of symmetry-enforced quantum materials.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an AI-accelerated framework using graph neural networks to discover symmetry-dictated quantum materials for antiferromagnetic spintronics. From over 100,000 configurations, the model identified 35 altermagnetic and 20 Tτ-antiferromagnetic candidates, demonstrating d-wave altermagnets with high spin-charge conversion and efficient Néel spin-orbit torque switching in intercalated transition metal dichalcogenides.
AI.motivation: The advancement of antiferromagnetic spintronics depends on quantum materials with target symmetry-dictated functionalities. However, their systematic discovery is hindered by the immense configurational complexity of the available material space, creating a major bottleneck in the field.
AI.method: The study employs a symmetry-guided AI-accelerated framework incorporating graph neural networks with high generalization ability. Using transfer learning from fully intercalated transition metal dichalcogenides (iTMDs), the model was trained on only 200 relaxed partially intercalated structures. This approach enabled efficient exploration of more than 100,000 partially intercalated configurations to identify ground-state candidates with specific symmetry properties.
AI.result: The framework identified 35 altermagnetic and 20 Tτ-antferromagnetic ground-state candidates. The study demonstrates that tuning spin-group symmetry through intercalant arrangement or magnetic ordering realizes a series of d-wave altermagnets in hexagonal systems with high spin-charge conversion efficiency. Additionally, plentiful Tτ-antiferromagnets were revealed that enable efficient Néel spin-orbit torque switching, driven by giant T-odd spin Edelstein susceptibilities.
AI.conclusion: These results establish iTMDs as a versatile platform for spintronics and provide a general strategy for the accelerated design of symmetry-enforced quantum materials. The AI framework enables rapid discovery of functional quantum materials that would be impractical to find through conventional experimental or computational methods.

title: Co-operating multiorbital and nonlocal correlations in bilayer nickelate
summary: The interplay of multiorbital physics and nonlocal self-energy effects is studied within an effective three-orbital model for the high-pressure normal state of superconducting bilayer nickelate La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$. The model is solved within an advanced many-body framework capturing $k$-dependent correlations beyond dynamical mean-field theory. Different low-energy scenarios subtly depend on the strength of the interorbital interaction, either placing the notorious flat $γ$ quasiparticle band in the occupied part of the spectrum, or letting it cross the Fermi level. In the latter case, intriguing spin-polaron formation due to the scattering of electrons with paramagnon excitations takes place. This leads to bound states appearing as a shadow band with incoherent low-energy spectral weight below the Fermi level. Our results uncover additional competing states that exist in bilayer nickelates and could explain the controversy of recent angle-resolved photoemission experiments.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the interplay of multiorbital physics and nonlocal self-energy effects in La₃Ni₂O₇ bilayer nickelates under high pressure using an advanced many-body method beyond dynamical mean-field theory.
AI.motivation: To understand the controversial angle-resolved photoemission (ARPES) experimental results in bilayer nickelates and resolve the debate about the location of the flat γ quasiparticle band relative to the Fermi level.
AI.method: Effective three-orbital model solved within an advanced many-body framework capturing k-dependent correlations beyond DMFT, allowing investigation of different low-energy scenarios based on interorbital interaction strength.
AI.result: Two distinct scenarios emerge: (1) the flat γ quasiparticle band sits in the occupied part of the spectrum, or (2) it crosses the Fermi level. When the γ band crosses the Fermi level, spin-polaron formation occurs due to scattering with paramagnon excitations, creating bound states (shadow band) with incoherent low-energy spectral weight below the Fermi level.
AI.conclusion: The results uncover additional competing states in bilayer nickelates and provide a theoretical framework that could explain the controversy in recent ARPES experiments, highlighting the importance of both multiorbital physics and nonlocal correlations in these materials.

title: Alkaline-Earth Rare-Earth Fluoride Nanoparticle Superlattices for Ultrafast, Radiation Stable Scintillators
summary: Radioluminescent nanostructures provide a pathway to the fabrication of next-generation scintillators with tunability in composition, size, and morphology, and spectral and temporal properties, as well as scalable processing. Here we create a 3D millimeter-scale solid-state scintillators from SrLuF Ce3+, Pr3+ (SrLuF) core-shell nanostructures, integrating nanoscale building blocks into self-assembled macroscopic crystals. These scintillators exhibit single-digit nanosecond decay times, linear response, resistance to radiation-induced degradation, and optical emission yields within an order of magnitude of YAG Ce3+. We select a SrLuF host lattice owing to its high effective atomic number, wide band gap, and low phonon energy, which together support efficient 4f-5d radiative transitions from Ce3+ and Pr3+ activators while suppressing afterglow. We create a library of core-shell nanoscintillators with undoped SrLuF shells and cores spanning compositions from undoped SrLuF to fully doped SrCeF or SrPrF. Time-resolved and steady-state X-ray excited optical luminescence (XEOL) reveal broadband emission at 310 nm (Ce3+) and 335 nm (Pr3+) with biexponential decays in the sub-nanosecond (100-500 ps) and sub-15 ns (4-13 ns) regimes, demonstrating tunable radiative efficiency and ultrafast dynamics. Ensemble performance of the mm-scale superlattices is characterized under both continuous-wave and femtosecond high-intensity excitation, revealing high light yield, linear response, and radiation hardness under extreme irradiation of ultrafast 50fs X-ray pulses up to 5mJ per mm2 corresponding to a peak intensity of 1013 W per cm2. Together, these results establish a design framework for stable, bright, and tunable scintillation platforms with applications in precision health, space exploration and hard X-ray imaging at next-generation free-electron laser facilities.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a novel approach to creating 3D millimeter-scale solid-state scintillators using SrLuF:Ce3+, Pr3+ core-shell nanostructures that can be self-assembled into macroscopic crystals. These scintillators demonstrate ultrafast decay times (sub-15 ns), high radiation hardness, and tunable emission properties, offering a new design framework for advanced scintillation platforms.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to develop next-generation scintillators with tunable composition, size, morphology, spectral and temporal properties, along with scalable processing capabilities. Traditional scintillators lack precise tunability and often suffer from radiation-induced degradation and afterglow. The researchers aimed to create a material platform that addresses these limitations while maintaining high efficiency and stability under extreme radiation conditions.
AI.method: The researchers created a library of core-shell nanoscintillators with undoped SrLuF shells and cores spanning compositions from undoped SrLuF to fully doped SrCeF or SrPrF. They selected SrLuF as the host lattice due to its high effective atomic number, wide band gap, and low phonon energy. These nanoscale building blocks were integrated into self-assembled millimeter-scale superlattices. The performance was characterized using time-resolved and steady-state X-ray excited optical luminescence (XEOL) under both continuous-wave and femtosecond high-intensity excitation conditions.
AI.result: The resulting scintillators exhibit single-digit nanosecond decay times, linear response, resistance to radiation-induced degradation, and optical emission yields within an order of magnitude of YAG:Ce3+. XEOL revealed broadband emission at 310 nm (Ce3+) and 335 nm (Pr3+) with biexponential decays in the sub-nanosecond (100-500 ps) and sub-15 ns (4-13 ns) regimes. The materials demonstrated radiation hardness under extreme irradiation of ultrafast 50fs X-ray pulses up to 5mJ per mm2 (peak intensity: 10^13 W/cm²).
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a design framework for stable, bright, and tunable scintillation platforms with applications in precision health (medical imaging), space exploration, and hard X-ray imaging at next-generation free-electron laser facilities. The core-shell nanostructure approach enables precise control over scintillation properties while maintaining excellent radiation hardness and fast response times, representing a significant advancement in scintillator technology.

title: Differentiable hybrid force fields support scalable autonomous electrolyte discovery
summary: Autonomous electrolyte discovery demands a computational engine that satisfies a critical trilemma: it must be fast enough for high-throughput screening, accurate enough for quantitative property prediction, and calibratable enough for online refinement. Classical empirical force fields (FFs) are fast but rely heavily on error cancellation, while standard machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) are computationally expensive, lack rigorous long-range physics, and resist gradient-based calibration. In this Perspective, we highlight that differentiable hybrid FFs resolve this trilemma by fusing physically motivated functional forms with neural-network short-range corrections. Grounded in Energy Decomposition Analysis (EDA), state-of-the-art models such as PhyNEO-Electrolyte and ByteFF-Pol achieve zero-shot generalization to bulk phases, delivering throughputs on the order of tens of ns/day (up to $\sim$50 ns/day, depending on model complexity) for 10,000-atom systems. Crucially, their physical skeletons provide a well-conditioned parameter space for differentiable molecular dynamics (dMD). This enables a dual-calibration paradigm: bottom-up \textit{ab initio} parameterization combined with top-down fine-tuning from macroscopic experimental observables. We propose that this architecture meets the requirements of a ``ChemRobot-ready'' digital twin by integrating physics-grounded simulation with experimentally calibratable refinement, thereby enabling closed-loop autonomous electrolyte discovery.

AI.tldr: This Perspective paper addresses a critical trilemma in autonomous electrolyte discovery: balancing computational speed, accuracy, and calibratability. The authors propose differentiable hybrid force fields that combine physically motivated functional forms with neural-network corrections, achieving ~50 ns/day throughput for 10,000-atom systems while enabling dual calibration from both ab initio and experimental data.
AI.motivation: Autonomous electrolyte discovery requires a computational engine that satisfies three competing demands: high-throughput screening speed, quantitative property prediction accuracy, and online refinement calibratability. Classical empirical force fields are fast but rely on error cancellation, while standard MLIPs are computationally expensive, lack rigorous long-range physics, and resist gradient-based calibration.
AI.method: The paper proposes differentiable hybrid force fields that fuse physically motivated functional forms with neural-network short-range corrections, grounded in Energy Decomposition Analysis (EDA). Examples include PhyNEO-Electrolyte and ByteFF-Pol models. This architecture enables dual-calibration: bottom-up ab initio parameterization combined with top-down fine-tuning from macroscopic experimental observables.
AI.result: State-of-the-art models achieve zero-shot generalization to bulk phases with throughputs on the order of tens of ns/day (~50 ns/day depending on model complexity) for 10,000-atom systems. Their physical skeletons provide well-conditioned parameter spaces for differentiable molecular dynamics (dMD).
AI.conclusion: This differentiable hybrid FF architecture meets requirements for a "ChemRobot-ready" digital twin by integrating physics-grounded simulation with experimentally calibratable refinement, enabling closed-loop autonomous electrolyte discovery.

title: Revealing the nature of the charge density wave order of ErTe$_3$ via Raman scattering under anisotropic strain
summary: The nature of charge density wave (CDW) order parameter of the tritelluride ErTe$_3$ is investigated by polarization-resolved Raman scattering under anisotropic strain. We show that the CDW amplitude mode can be used to track the mirror-symmetry breakings associated with the CDW order. The mirror-symmetry breakings are found to track each other as a function of strain and temperature arguing against the recently proposed ferro-axial multi-component order. Instead, we show that a single component CDW order parameter with an ordering wavevector tilted away from the principle crystallographic axis can reproduce the observed mirror symmetry breakings and their manifestation in the symmetry-resolved Raman spectra.

AI.tldr: This study uses polarization-resolved Raman scattering under anisotropic strain to investigate the nature of CDW order in ErTe3. The results show that mirror-symmetry breakings track each other with strain and temperature, contradicting the proposed ferro-axial multi-component order. Instead, a single-component CDW order with a tilted wavevector successfully explains the observations.
AI.motivation: The study aims to resolve the controversy regarding the nature of the charge density wave (CDW) order parameter in ErTe3. Specifically, it investigates whether the CDW order involves a multi-component (ferro-axial) structure or a single-component order with a tilted wavevector, as recent proposals suggested a complex multi-component order.
AI.method: Polarization-resolved Raman scattering under anisotropic strain was employed to investigate the CDW amplitude mode and track mirror-symmetry breakings associated with the CDW order. The measurements were performed as a function of both strain and temperature.
AI.result: The mirror-symmetry breakings were found to track each other consistently as a function of strain and temperature, which contradicts the ferro-axial multi-component order scenario. The experimental data successfully reproduced the observed mirror symmetry breakings and their manifestation in symmetry-resolved Raman spectra using a single-component CDW order parameter with an ordering wavevector tilted away from the principal crystallographic axis.
AI.conclusion: The study argues against the ferro-axial multi-component order scenario and demonstrates that a single-component CDW order parameter with a tilted ordering wavevector can explain the observed mirror symmetry breakings. This finding provides a simpler and more consistent description of the CDW nature in ErTe3, reconciling the experimental observations with a unified theoretical framework.

title: Tailoring the Optoelectronic, Photocatalytic, Thermoelectric and Thermodynamic Properties of Halides Li2InBiX6 (X = Cl, Br, I) for Energy Conversion: A DFT Study
summary: Double perovskite halides are emerging as promising materials for a wide range of applications, particularly in renewable energy technologies such as solar cell devices, thereby contributing to addressing global energy demands. In this work, the structural, electronic, optical, dielectric, thermoelectric, and photocatalytic properties of Li2InBiX6 (X = Cl, Br, I) halides are systematically investigated using density functional theory. The calculated formation energies confirm the thermodynamic stability of these compounds in the cubic phase. The studied materials exhibit semiconducting behavior with direct bandgaps of 1.7 eV, 1.3 eV, and 1.1 eV for Li2InBiCl6, Li2InBiBr6, and Li2InBiI6, respectively. The complex dielectric function is analyzed to explore their optical response, revealing strong absorption in the infrared and visible regions, indicating suitability for optoelectronic applications. Thermoelectric properties, including the Seebeck coefficient, electrical conductivity, and figure of merit (ZT), are evaluated over a temperature range of 30 to 800 K. The relatively small bandgaps contribute to enhanced thermoelectric performance, reflected in improved power factors. Furthermore, photocatalytic analysis indicates that Li2InBiX6 compounds are suitable candidates for water oxidation reactions within the pH range of 0 to 7. Overall, the combined thermoelectric and optical performance highlights these double perovskite halides as promising materials for future energy conversion applications.
