title: Emergence of Lissajous trajectories in skyrmion oscillator
summary: Understanding the dynamics of current-driven skyrmion is essential for their practical applications. In this study, we apply an AC current pulse (a) in x-- direction, and (b) in both x-- and y-- directions through the free layer of a Co/Pt thin film and investigate the motion of the skyrmion. We show that the skyrmion follows the sinusoidal current pulse and behaves like a forced oscillator in the range of current amplitude 1 $\times$ 10$^{11}$ A/m$^2$ to 1 $\times$ 10$^{12}$ A/m$^2$ and frequency 5 $\times$ 10$^{8}$ Hz to 1 $\times$ 10$^{10}$ Hz. For current pulse of (A$_1$sin$ω_1$t, A$_2$sin($ω_2$t+$φ$), 0), the skyrmion forms Lissajous figures in the x-y plane, same as observed in classical mechanics. The results are compared at T = 0 K and T $>$ 0 K to analyze the effect of temperature. As the skyrmion Hall angle ($θ_{SkH}$) and stochastic thermal fluctuation ($\textbf{F}^{Th}$) are functions of temperature, the skyrmion starts deviating from its path at T = 0 K with increasing temperature and eventually generates somewhat deformed Lissajous figures from ideal.

AI.tldr: This study investigates current-driven skyrmion dynamics in Co/Pt thin films by applying AC current pulses in different directions. The skyrmion behaves as a forced oscillator and forms Lissajous figures, with temperature causing deviations from ideal paths due to thermal fluctuations and skyrmion Hall angle effects.
AI.motivation: Understanding the dynamics of current-driven skyrmions is essential for their practical applications in magnetic memory and logic devices.
AI.method: Applied AC current pulses (a) in x-direction and (b) in both x and y directions through the free layer of Co/Pt thin film; investigated skyrmion motion under current amplitude 1×10^11 to 1×10^12 A/m^2 and frequency 5×10^8 to 1×10^10 Hz; compared results at T=0 K and T>0 K.
AI.result: Skyrmion follows sinusoidal current pulse and behaves like a forced oscillator; for elliptical current (A1sinω1t, A2sin(ω2t+φ), 0), skyrmion forms Lissajous figures similar to classical mechanics; at T>0 K, skyrmion deviates from ideal path due to temperature-dependent skyrmion Hall angle and stochastic thermal fluctuations, resulting in deformed Lissajous figures.
AI.conclusion: Temperature significantly affects skyrmion motion, causing deviations from ideal Lissajous trajectories at higher temperatures due to thermal fluctuations and skyrmion Hall angle effects.

title: Oxophilic Silver-Based Nanoparticles with Low Pd-Au Loading for Ethanol and Glycerol Electrooxidation in Alkaline Media
summary: The electrocatalytic activity of oxophilic Ag nanoparticles, combined with small amounts of Pd and Au, was investigated for ethanol oxidation reactions (EOR) and glycerol oxidation reactions (GOR) in alkaline media. The EOR and GOR results revealed competitive current densities and less positive onset potentials for the AgPd/C and AgPdAu/C electrocatalysts, both containing 5 wt% Pd, compared to the commercial Pd/C catalyst, which has a significantly higher loading of the costly noble metal (20 wt%). In situ FTIR analyses during EOR confirmed that ethanol is initially adsorbed as acetylated species, which are subsequently oxidized to acetate ions, the main stable product in alkaline medium. However, the incorporation of Pd and Au into the Ag matrix did not significantly alter the reaction mechanism. During GOR, the in situ FTIR studies demonstrated that catalyst composition influences the oxidation pathways: Pd-rich surfaces favor oxalate formation, while a significant presence of Ag promotes deeper oxidation (up to carbonate), with the AgPdAu ternary catalyst exhibiting intermediate behavior. One key benefit is the lower susceptibility of Ag to irreversible adsorption of reaction byproducts, which enhances electrocatalyst durability. Thus, surface segregation of Ag at high potentials can modify the catalytic surface reactivity, affecting both stability and efficiency.

AI.tldr: This study investigates Ag-based nanoparticle electrocatalysts (Ag, AgPd, and AgPdAu) for ethanol and glycerol oxidation in alkaline media. The ternary AgPdAu/C catalyst with only 5 wt% Pd achieves comparable or better performance than commercial 20 wt% Pd/C, while offering improved durability due to Ag's resistance to irreversible byproduct adsorption.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to develop more cost-effective and durable electrocatalysts for alcohol oxidation reactions by reducing the high loading of expensive noble metals (Pd) while maintaining or improving electrocatalytic performance. Traditional Pd-based catalysts suffer from irreversible adsorption of reaction byproducts, limiting their practical applications in fuel cells.
AI.method: The researchers synthesized Ag/C, AgPd/C (with 5 wt% Pd), and AgPdAu/C (with 5 wt% Pd and Au) catalysts, then characterized their electrocatalytic activity toward ethanol oxidation reaction (EOR) and glycerol oxidation reaction (GOR) in alkaline media. They used in situ FTIR spectroscopy to identify reaction intermediates and products, revealing how catalyst composition affects reaction pathways.
AI.result: The key results show: (1) AgPd/C and AgPdAu/C with only 5 wt% Pd achieve competitive current densities and less positive onset potentials compared to commercial 20 wt% Pd/C. (2) For EOR, ethanol is oxidized to acetate ions as the main product, with Pd/Au incorporation not significantly changing the mechanism. (3) For GOR, catalyst composition critically influences pathways—Pd-rich surfaces produce oxalate, Ag-rich surfaces enable deeper oxidation to carbonate, with AgPdAu showing intermediate behavior. (4) Ag-based catalysts show lower susceptibility to irreversible adsorption of byproducts, enhancing durability.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion is that Ag-based electrocatalysts with minimal Pd addition (5 wt%) represent a promising alternative to conventional high-Pd catalysts for alcohol oxidation in alkaline media. The ternary AgPdAu/C catalyst offers an optimal balance of activity, durability, and cost-effectiveness. The study also highlights how surface segregation of Ag at high potentials can be leveraged to modify catalytic surface reactivity, providing a strategy for designing more efficient and stable electrocatalysts for renewable energy applications.

title: Unveiling the Core of Materials Properties via SISSO and Sensitivity Analysis
summary: Interpretable AI can reveal physical principles governing intricate materials properties by uncovering explicit relationships between physical parameters and target properties. The sure-independence screening and sparsifying operator (SISSO) symbolic-regression approach identifies analytical expressions that correlate a target property with a small set of parameters, termed materials genes, selected from a large pool of candidates. However, multiple gene combinations can yield equally accurate SISSO models, with individual genes contributing with different weights. Here, we establish a derivative-based sensitivity analysis that resolves the non-uniqueness of symbolic-regression descriptions, enhances interpretability, thereby enabling deeper physical insight. This analysis reveals how distinct gene combinations encode equivalent information and identifies valence orbital radii, nuclear charges, and their products as the key quantities governing the equilibrium lattice constant of perovskites.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a derivative-based sensitivity analysis to resolve non-uniqueness in SISSO symbolic regression for materials discovery, successfully identifying valence orbital radii, nuclear charges, and their products as key parameters governing perovskite lattice constants.
AI.motivation: Multiple gene combinations can yield equally accurate SISSO models, with individual genes contributing differently, leading to non-uniqueness problems that hinder interpretability and physical insight.
AI.method: The study employs a derivative-based sensitivity analysis combined with SISSO symbolic-regression approach to analyze gene contributions and weights in predictive models.
AI.result: The analysis reveals that valence orbital radii, nuclear charges, and their products are key quantities governing the equilibrium lattice constant of perovskites.
AI.conclusion: The derivative-based sensitivity analysis successfully resolves non-uniqueness in symbolic regression descriptions, identifies key quantities (valence orbital radii, nuclear charges, and their products) governing perovskite lattice constants, and enables deeper physical insight into how different gene combinations encode equivalent information.

title: Equivariant Many-body Message Passing Interatomic Potentials for Magnetic Materials
summary: Magnetism governs key properties of materials used in energy, data storage, and spintronic technologies, yet its complex coupling to lattice and electronic degrees of freedom challenges conventional first-principles approaches. We introduce an equivariant message-passing graph neural network that embeds atomic magnetic moments as explicit degrees of freedom, enabling the learning of magnetic interactions beyond collinear approximations. The model learns physically consistent and transferable representations of magnetic behaviour and can incorporate spin-orbit coupling, achieving near density-functional-theory accuracy with strong data efficiency across diverse magnetic systems by fine-tuning from a pre-trained model. Applications to structural transformations, finite-temperature magnetic phenomena, and materials screening for strongly spin-orbit coupled materials demonstrate transferable magnetic behaviour, establishing a practical foundation for data-driven, high-throughput discovery of complex magnetic materials.

AI.tldr: An equivariant message-passing graph neural network that embeds atomic magnetic moments as explicit degrees of freedom, enabling learning of magnetic interactions beyond collinear approximations with near DFT accuracy and strong data efficiency.
AI.motivation: Magnetism is crucial for energy, data storage, and spintronic technologies, but its complex coupling to lattice and electronic degrees of freedom challenges conventional first-principles approaches.
AI.method: Introduced an equivariant message-passing graph neural network that embeds atomic magnetic moments as explicit degrees of freedom, enabling learning beyond collinear approximations. The model incorporates spin-orbit coupling and achieves transferability through fine-tuning from a pre-trained model.
AI.result: Achieved near density-functional-theory accuracy with strong data efficiency across diverse magnetic systems. Demonstrated applications to structural transformations, finite-temperature magnetic phenomena, and materials screening for strongly spin-orbit coupled materials.
AI.conclusion: The model establishes a practical foundation for data-driven, high-throughput discovery of complex magnetic materials by providing transferable magnetic behavior representations.

title: Switching magnetic spin-states using small magnetic fields in compositionally complex Sm(M7)O$_3$
summary: High-entropy perovskites (HEPs) offer a unique platform for exploring magnetic phenomena arising from extreme B-site chemical disorder. In Sm(M7)O$_3$, where there are 7 cations in equal amounts at the B-site; M = Ti, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu), we observe long-range antiferromagnetic ordering near 105 K accompanied by a small but robust excess magnetic moment intrinsic to the chemically disordered lattice. This uncompensated moment is evident from ZFC-FC irreversibility, shifts in the isothermal M(H) loops, and discrete remanent states identified through direct-current-demagnetization measurements. Remarkably, cooling fields as small as $\pm$ 20 Oe are sufficient to select the direction of the excess moment, and the chosen magnetic state remains stable against applied fields up to 50 kOe. A low-temperature anomaly in the remanent magnetization further reveals a secondary contribution from the Sm$^{3+}$ sublattice, although the primary origin of the excess moment resides in the B-site AFM sublattice.

AI.tldr: High-entropy perovskite Sm(M7)O3 with 7 cations at the B-site exhibits long-range antiferromagnetic ordering at 105 K with a small but robust excess magnetic moment arising from chemical disorder. Small cooling fields (±20 Oe) can select the direction of this moment, and it remains stable up to 50 kOe. The primary excess moment originates from the B-site AFM sublattice, with a secondary contribution from the Sm3+ sublattice at low temperatures.
AI.motivation: This study explores magnetic phenomena in high-entropy perovskites (HEPs) arising from extreme B-site chemical disorder. The motivation is to investigate how chemical disorder at the B-site influences magnetic ordering and creates new magnetic phenomena in oxide systems.
AI.method: The researchers synthesized Sm(M7)O3 with 7 cations (Ti, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu) in equal amounts at the B-site. They characterized the magnetic properties using ZFC-FC measurements, isothermal M(H) loops, and direct-current-demagnetization measurements to identify remanent states and magnetic behavior.
AI.result: The study observed long-range antiferromagnetic ordering near 105 K with a small but robust excess magnetic moment intrinsic to the chemically disordered lattice. This uncompensated moment was evidenced by ZFC-FC irreversibility, shifts in M(H) loops, and discrete remanent states. Remarkably, cooling fields as small as ±20 Oe could select the direction of the excess moment, and this state remained stable against applied fields up to 50 kOe. A low-temperature anomaly revealed a secondary contribution from the Sm3+ sublattice, with the primary origin of the excess moment residing in the B-site AFM sublattice.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that high-entropy perovskites offer a unique platform for exploring magnetic phenomena arising from extreme chemical disorder. The B-site AFM sublattice generates a small but robust uncompensated moment that can be controlled by small cooling fields, remaining stable against large applied fields. This work suggests that chemical disorder in perovskites can produce novel magnetic states not found in conventional ordered materials.

title: Bulk versus interface nucleation of CO$_2$ hydrates from computer simulations
summary: Gas hydrates are of great relevance to both the oil industry and the environment. Understanding how these solid structures nucleate from aqueous solutions is essential to controlling their formation. Experimental studies have often suggested that hydrate nucleation originates at the interface between the aqueous phase and the guest-molecule reservoir. To assess this hypothesis, we perform molecular dynamics simulations of CO$_2$ hydrate nucleation. First, we place hydrate seeds at different positions relative to the interface and monitor their evolution, finding that seeds embedded in the bulk are more likely to grow than those located near or at the interface. Second, we analyse spontaneous nucleation simulations with and without an interface. Our previous work showed that nucleation rates are indistinguishable in both systems, strongly indicating that the interface does not play a role. Here, trajectory analysis reveals that hydrates nucleate in regions of locally high CO$_2$ concentration, which arise spontaneously in the bulk and are not associated with the interface. Our results indicate that hydrate nucleation does not preferentially occur at the interface, at least at the at deep supercooling conditions explored in this work. Further work at higher temperatures, and considering alternative nucleation locations, is needed to reconcile experiments and simulations, and thereby reach a deep understanding of the mechanism of hydrate formation.

AI.tldr: This study uses molecular dynamics simulations to investigate whether CO2 hydrate nucleation preferentially occurs at the interface between aqueous phase and guest-molecule reservoir. Results show hydrate seeds in the bulk grow more readily than those near the interface, and spontaneous nucleation occurs in regions of high CO2 concentration in the bulk rather than at the interface, challenging the interfacial nucleation hypothesis.
AI.motivation: Experimental studies suggest hydrate nucleation originates at the interface between aqueous phase and guest-molecule reservoir. This hypothesis was tested to understand the fundamental mechanism of hydrate formation relevant to oil industry and environmental applications.
AI.method: Molecular dynamics simulations of CO2 hydrate nucleation were performed in two ways: (1) placing hydrate seeds at different positions relative to the interface and monitoring evolution, and (2) analyzing spontaneous nucleation simulations with and without an interface.
AI.result: Seeds embedded in the bulk are more likely to grow than those located near or at the interface. Nucleation rates are indistinguishable in systems with and without an interface. Trajectory analysis reveals hydrates nucleate in regions of locally high CO2 concentration that arise spontaneously in the bulk and are not associated with the interface.
AI.conclusion: Hydrate nucleation does not preferentially occur at the interface under deep supercooling conditions explored. Further work at higher temperatures and considering alternative nucleation locations is needed to reconcile experiments and simulations for a complete understanding of hydrate formation.

title: Giant photostriction in lead-free ferroelectric stemming from photo-excited thermalized carriers
summary: Ferroelectrics are polar materials whose polarization can be switched by applying electric fields; they offer unique opportunities to develop performant photostrictive materials, i.e., materials that can deform under visible light illumination. Naturally devoid of inversion symmetry, they exhibit original photogalvanic effects such as the Bulk Photovoltaic Effect, which relies on ``hot'' photoexcited carriers. It has long been thought that the electric field generated by this effect may couple to the natural piezoelectric abilities of ferroelectrics to provide large photoinduced deformations. However, due to competing effects, such as thermal dilatation, deformation potential, polarization, or depolarizing-field screening by \textit{thermalized} carriers, it remains unclear which microscopic phenomena govern the photoinduced deformations in classical ferroelectric materials. Here, we demonstrate the largest photoinduced deformation measured in a ferroelectric thin film. Reaching 1 %, this giant photostriction likely originates from the contribution of thermalized photoinduced carriers.

AI.tldr: This paper reports the largest photoinduced deformation (1%) ever measured in a ferroelectric thin film, demonstrating giant photostriction that likely originates from thermalized photoinduced carriers.
AI.motivation: Ferroelectrics offer opportunities for photostrictive materials that deform under visible light due to the Bulk Photovoltaic Effect. It was long thought that the electric field from this effect could couple with piezoelectric abilities to produce large deformations, but competing effects made it unclear which phenomena govern these deformations in classical ferroelectric materials.
AI.method: The researchers measured photoinduced deformation in a ferroelectric thin film under visible light illumination.
AI.result: They achieved a giant photostriction of 1%, the largest photoinduced deformation ever measured in a ferroelectric thin film.
AI.conclusion: This giant photostriction likely originates from the contribution of thermalized photoinduced carriers, providing insight into the microscopic phenomena governing photoinduced deformations in ferroelectric materials.

title: Odd-parity Magnetism from the Generalized Bloch Theorem
summary: In the non-relativistic limit, helimagnetic order is always associated with odd-parity magnetism. That is, for single-particle states the expectation value of the electronic spin is odd in crystal momentum, which implies direct control of the spin by means of electric fields. However, the theoretical description of helimagnets is hindered by the fact that the spiral pitch may require large super cells or even be incommensurate with the lattice. In the this letter we show that such issues may be remedied by use of the Generalized Bloch theorem. It allows one to describe (by models or first principles) the system in terms of the primitive unit cell, from which all relevant properties can be obtained by downfolding in reciprocal space. We exemplify the procedure using MnI$_2$ and NiI$_2$, which are known type II multiferroics having spiral order and the helimagnetic metal MnTe$_2$. We analyze how the magnitude of spin splitting depends on orbital composition of bands, and we show that spin splitting is maximized for states having large odd-orbital ($p$-type) character. It is straightforward to generalize the framework to handle response functions for helimagnets using only the primitive unit cell and the present downfolding procedure thus strongly facilitate theoretical progress in the field.

AI.tldr: The Generalized Bloch theorem enables efficient modeling of helimagnetic systems using primitive unit cells instead of large supercells, demonstrating that spin splitting is maximized for p-orbital states in MnI2, NiI2, and MnTe2.
AI.motivation: Theoretical description of helimagnets is hindered by the need for large supercells or incommensurate spiral pitch. Helimagnetic order is associated with odd-parity magnetism allowing direct spin control by electric fields, but practical modeling is challenging.
AI.method: The Generalized Bloch theorem is used to describe the system in terms of the primitive unit cell, with properties obtained by downfolding in reciprocal space. The procedure is exemplified using MnI2, NiI2 (type II multiferroics with spiral order) and the helimagnetic metal MnTe2.
AI.result: Spin splitting depends on orbital composition of bands and is maximized for states with large odd-orbital (p-type) character.
AI.conclusion: The framework can be generalized to handle response functions for helimagnets using only the primitive unit cell, facilitating theoretical progress in the field.

title: Rapid and Highly Efficient Synergistic Sonophotocatalytic Degradation of Methyl Orange with CuDoped LaFeO3 Perovskite Nanoparticles
summary: The integration of sonocatalysis with photocatalysis offers a powerful strategy for advanced wastewater treatment by overcoming rapid charge carrier recombination in conventional photocatalytic systems. Although these processes are often treated separately due to their distinct mechanisms, their combination creates a highly efficient synergistic system. In this study, we investigate the sonophotocatalytic degradation of methyl orange (MO) using Cu-doped LaFeO3 perovskite nanoparticles. The Cu doped catalyst demonstrated excellent performance, achieving a degradation rate of 0.0455 min-1 and complete removal of MO within 120 minutes under combined ultrasonic and light irradiation. A strong synergistic effect was observed, with a synergy index of approximately 10, highlighting the enhanced interaction between sonocatalysis and photocatalysis. The catalyst also exhibited good stability and reusability, maintaining high efficiency over four consecutive cycles. Mechanistic studies using scavenger experiments revealed that hydroxyl radicals and photogenerated holes are the main reactive species responsible for degradation. A plausible reaction pathway is proposed based on these findings. Overall, Cu doped LaFeO3 shows superior sonophotocatalytic activity compared to the undoped material, demonstrating the potential of synergistic sonophotocatalytic processes for efficient pollutant removal.

AI.tldr: This study explores Cu-doped LaFeO3 perovskite nanoparticles for sonophotocatalytic degradation of methyl orange, achieving complete removal within 120 minutes with a synergistic effect index of ~10, demonstrating potential for efficient wastewater treatment.
AI.motivation: This research aims to overcome rapid charge carrier recombination in conventional photocatalytic systems by integrating sonocatalysis with photocatalysis. Although these processes are often treated separately due to their distinct mechanisms, their combination creates a highly efficient synergistic system for advanced wastewater treatment.
AI.method: The study investigated sonophotocatalytic degradation of methyl orange (MO) using Cu-doped LaFeO3 perovskite nanoparticles under combined ultrasonic and light irradiation. Mechanistic studies were conducted using scavenger experiments to identify the main reactive species involved in the degradation process.
AI.result: The Cu-doped catalyst achieved a degradation rate of 0.0455 min-1 and complete removal of MO within 120 minutes under combined ultrasonic and light irradiation. A strong synergistic effect was observed with a synergy index of approximately 10. The catalyst maintained high efficiency over four consecutive cycles. Mechanistic studies revealed that hydroxyl radicals and photogenerated holes are the main reactive species responsible for degradation.
AI.conclusion: Cu-doped LaFeO3 demonstrates superior sonophotocatalytic activity compared to undoped material, with good stability and reusability over four consecutive cycles. The process shows excellent potential for efficient pollutant removal in wastewater treatment applications.

title: 2D Ferroelectric Ruddlesden-Popper Perovskites: an Emerging Fully Electronically Controllable Shift Current and Persistent Spin Helix
summary: Two-dimensional (2D) hybrid organic--inorganic perovskites (HOIPs) are promising candidates for next-generation optoelectronic and spintronic applications. This work systematically investigates the relationship between structural distortions and functional responses in three $C_{2v}$-symmetric Ruddlesden--Popper (RP) ferroelectric perovskites, $(4,4\text{-DFPD})_{2}\mathrm{PbI}_{4}$, $(\mathrm{DFCHA})_{2}\mathrm{PbI}_{4}$, and PEPI, using first-principles calculations combined with irreducible representation decomposition and wave-vector point-group symmetry (WPGS) analysis. The results reveal that the lead--iodide framework yields shift-current (SC) magnitudes comparable to, and in specific cases even an order of magnitude larger than, those of traditional ferroelectric oxides, with PEPI reaching a maximum of $69.16\ μ\mathrm{A}/\mathrm{V}^{2}$. The SC magnitude correlates positively with the octahedral distortion index ($D_i$), while a competition mechanism is identified between covalent bond strength and structural asymmetry, where increased average bond lengths can offset the enhancement induced by $D_i$. Regarding spintronics, $C_{2v}$ symmetry-protected persistent spin textures (PST) are identified. A transition to $C_2$-protected quasi-PST occurs in monoclinic $(4,4\text{-DFHHA})_{2}\mathrm{PbI}_{4}$, leading to a persistent spin helix (PSH) with long-distance spin transport. The synergy among ferroelectricity, SC, and PST enables nonvolatile electrical control of both photocurrent direction and spin configurations. This work provides evaluation criteria and practical guidance for designing high-performance integrated spintronic--photovoltaic devices.

AI.tldr: This work investigates structural distortions and functional responses in three C2v-symmetric Ruddlesden-Popper ferroelectric perovskites using first-principles calculations. The lead-iodide framework shows shift-current magnitudes comparable or larger than traditional ferroelectric oxides, with PEPI reaching 69.16 μA/V². The research identifies C2v symmetry-protected persistent spin textures and demonstrates synergy among ferroelectricity, shift-current, and PST for nonvolatile electrical control of photocurrent direction and spin configurations.
AI.motivation: Two-dimensional hybrid organic-inorganic perovskites (HOIPs) are promising candidates for next-generation optoelectronic and spintronic applications, but the relationship between structural distortions and functional responses needs systematic investigation.
AI.method: First-principles calculations combined with irreducible representation decomposition and wave-vector point-group symmetry (WPGS) analysis.
AI.result: The lead-iodide framework yields shift-current (SC) magnitudes comparable to, and in specific cases even an order of magnitude larger than, those of traditional ferroelectric oxides, with PEPI reaching a maximum of 69.16 μA/V². The SC magnitude correlates positively with the octahedral distortion index (Di), while a competition mechanism is identified between covalent bond strength and structural asymmetry. C2v symmetry-protected persistent spin textures (PST) are identified, and a transition to C2-protected quasi-PST occurs in monoclinic (4,4-DFHHA)2PbI4, leading to a persistent spin helix (PSH) with long-distance spin transport.
AI.conclusion: The synergy among ferroelectricity, SC, and PST enables nonvolatile electrical control of both photocurrent direction and spin configurations. This work provides evaluation criteria and practical guidance for designing high-performance integrated spintronic-photovoltaic devices.

title: Comparative high-pressure study on rare-earth entropy fluorite-type oxides
summary: We report a comparative high-pressure study of two fluorite-type rare-earth oxides with increasing configurational entropy, (CePr)O$_{2-δ}$ and (CePrLa)O$_{2-δ}$. Synchrotron-based powder X-ray diffraction and Raman spectroscopy were carried out up to 30 GPa and 20 GPa, respectively. Both compounds retain the cubic fluorite structure throughout the pressure range explored, although an anomaly is observed between 9-16 GPa, characterized by a compressibility plateau and changes in vibrational modes. This behavior is attributed to local lattice distortions and a progressive bond angle bending rather than abrupt phase transitions. In (CePrLa)O$_{2-δ}$, the onset of amorphization is observed above 22 GPa, highlighting its reduced structural stability. The bulk modulus of both systems shows a slight decrease after the onset of the anomaly, suggesting subtle lattice softening. Raman spectroscopy reveals suppression of the F$_{2g}$ mode intensity with increasing cationic disorder, and under compression, partial reordering is evidenced by an increase in the RE-O mode intensity. Our results highlight the complex interplay between configurational entropy, cation size, and pressure in determining the structural stability and vibrational properties of rare-earth high-entropy oxides and provide insight into the mechanisms governing their resilience and local disorder under extreme conditions.

AI.tldr: High-pressure study up to 30 GPa of fluorite-type rare-earth oxides (CePr)O2-δ and (CePrLa)O2-δ reveals structural stability with an anomaly between 9-16 GPa due to local lattice distortions, and onset of amorphization above 22 GPa in the higher entropy compound.
AI.motivation: Understanding how configurational entropy influences structural stability and vibrational properties of rare-earth high-entropy oxides under extreme pressure conditions.
AI.method: Synchrotron-based powder X-ray diffraction (up to 30 GPa) and Raman spectroscopy (up to 20 GPa) were used to study two fluorite-type rare-earth oxides with increasing configurational entropy.
AI.result: Both compounds retain cubic fluorite structure throughout pressure range. An anomaly between 9-16 GPa shows compressibility plateau and vibrational mode changes due to local lattice distortions and bond angle bending. (CePrLa)O2-δ shows amorphization above 22 GPa. Bulk modulus decreases after anomaly onset. Raman shows F2g mode intensity suppression with cationic disorder and partial reordering under compression.
AI.conclusion: Configurational entropy, cation size, and pressure interact complexly to determine structural stability and vibrational properties. Higher entropy reduces structural stability under compression. Local disorder and lattice softening occur before amorphization.

title: Theory-Guided Discovery of Pressure-Induced Transitions in Fast-Ion Conductor BaSnF4
summary: Fast-ion conductors such as BaSnF4 are of significant interest for next-generation solid-state battery technologies due to their high ionic conductivity and chemical stability. However, the behaviour of these materials under extreme conditions remains poorly understood, despite the relevance of pressure-induced modifications for tuning functional properties. In this study, we combine density functional theory (DFT) calculations with high-pressure experiments to investigate the structural evolution of BaSnF4 up to 40 GPa. DFT predicts two pressure-induced phase transitions: from the ambient-pressure tetragonal P4/nmm phase to a monoclinic P21/m-I structure at 10 GPa, and subsequently to a denser monoclinic P21/m-II phase at 32 GPa. The first transition is experimentally confirmed via angle-dispersive X-ray diffraction, Raman spectroscopy, and electrical resistivity measurements, all performed at ambient temperature. The second transition is supported by distinct changes in high-pressure Raman modes and resistivity behaviour, consistent with a further structural reorganization. These findings not only clarify the high-pressure phase diagram of BaSnF4, but also shed light on the potential for pressure-tuned ionic transport in fluorostannate-based solid electrolytes.

AI.tldr: This study investigates structural evolution of BaSnF4 under high pressure up to 40 GPa using DFT calculations and experiments, discovering two pressure-induced phase transitions relevant for solid-state battery electrolytes.
AI.motivation: Fast-ion conductors like BaSnF4 are promising for next-generation solid-state batteries, but their behavior under extreme conditions (high pressure) remains poorly understood despite the importance of pressure for tuning functional properties.
AI.method: Combined density functional theory (DFT) calculations with high-pressure experiments including angle-dispersive X-ray diffraction, Raman spectroscopy, and electrical resistivity measurements at ambient temperature.
AI.result: DFT predicts two phase transitions: tetragonal P4/nmm → monoclinic P21/m-I at 10 GPa, then to denser monoclinic P21/m-II at 32 GPa. First transition experimentally confirmed; second supported by Raman and resistivity changes.
AI.conclusion: Findings clarify the high-pressure phase diagram of BaSnF4 and provide insights into pressure-tuned ionic transport in fluorostannate-based solid electrolytes.
